opportunities.title: Widget library
opportunities.summary: The small reusable collection where Philo keeps widgets that earned a permanent place.
opportunities.audience: people learning Philo's planning vocabulary
bestFor: People who want a fast definition of the reusable widget layer
notFor: People who need a comparison or setup guide

faqs.question: Why keep the library small?
faqs.answer: Because Philo treats permanence as something a widget earns after proving useful.

opportunities.title: Philo vs heavy project management tools
opportunities.summary: Why Philo fits lightweight personal execution loops better than heavyweight structured systems.
opportunities.audience: people deciding between a planner and a large work-management tool
opportunities.primaryQuery: philo vs heavy project management tools
bestFor: People who do not want a giant workspace just to run daily work
Users who want a local markdown-centered execution loop
notFor: Teams needing strict project process and enterprise workflow control
Organizations choosing a shared system of record

faqs.question: Is Philo meant to be a big workspace replacement?
faqs.answer: No. The product direction is explicitly about keeping the planning loop short, not building another giant workspace.

faqs.question: When do heavyweight tools still win?
faqs.answer: They win when the problem is a shared project system, not a personal note-driven execution loop.

relatedLinks.label: Write, decide, and run in one place

comparisonRows.question: You want a calm personal planning loop
comparisonRows.philo: Strong fit because the note itself stays central.
comparisonRows.alternative: Can feel heavy if you only need personal daily execution.

comparisonRows.question: You want structured shared workflows and fields everywhere
comparisonRows.philo: Less ideal. That is not the core bet.

comparisonRows.question: You want one-off tools created inline
comparisonRows.philo: Fits the disposable widget model well.
comparisonRows.alternative: Usually requires more setup and permanence.

opportunities.title: Use Philo beside an Obsidian vault
opportunities.summary: When an existing vault is still the source of truth, but you want a tighter planning loop.
opportunities.audience: people already invested in Obsidian
opportunities.primaryQuery: use philo beside an obsidian vault
bestFor: People who already store notes in an Obsidian vault
Users who want execution features without abandoning markdown ownership
notFor: People looking for a cloud migration path
Workflows that are not built around a vault

faqs.question: Does Philo force an Obsidian user to migrate?
faqs.answer: No. Philo is meant to sit beside your vault, not replace it.

faqs.question: What does Philo add on top?
faqs.answer: It focuses on keeping planning, unfinished work, recurrence, and lightweight tools closer to the note itself.

opportunities.title: Using Philo with an Obsidian vault

faqs.question: Can Philo work with an existing vault?
faqs.answer: Yes. Philo stores notes as markdown on disk and is meant to sit beside an existing Obsidian vault rather than replace it.

faqs.question: Why would an Obsidian user add Philo?
faqs.answer: Philo focuses on the gap between capture and execution, especially around task rollover, recurrence, and inline widgets.

opportunities.title: Disposable widgets in notes
opportunities.summary: How Philo treats one-off tools as cheap, inline widgets instead of heavyweight software projects.
opportunities.audience: people who need temporary tools while planning
bestFor: People who want calculators, trackers, or small tools inline in a note
Anyone who wants creation to be cheap and deletion to be safe
notFor: People who only need static text and checklists
Teams looking for a permanent low-code platform

faqs.question: Why make widgets disposable by default?
faqs.answer: Because many note-adjacent tools are only useful for a day or a week and should not carry a permanent setup tax.

faqs.question: Can useful widgets be saved?
faqs.answer: Yes. Widgets that earn their place can be saved into a small reusable library.

opportunities.title: Quick tools inside notes
opportunities.summary: When inline widgets are better than opening or building a separate tool.
opportunities.audience: people solving temporary problems while planning
bestFor: People who need a one-off tracker, calculator, or checklist while planning
Users who want a widget to live in the same note as the decision it supports
notFor: People who only want reusable permanent tools
Teams that need a broad internal tooling platform

faqs.question: What kinds of tools fit this model?
faqs.answer: Temporary trackers, calculators, experiments, and small planning utilities fit the disposable widget model well.

faqs.question: What happens when a quick tool becomes useful long term?
faqs.answer: Philo lets you save the winners into a small reusable library instead of committing every experiment up front.

bestFor: People who already use daily notes as their planning surface

notFor: People who never plan from notes

faqs.question: Why not just use a plain markdown file?
faqs.answer: A plain file captures ideas well, but it usually makes you manually rebuild rollover, recurrence, and context every day.

faqs.question: What changes with Philo?
faqs.answer: Philo keeps the markdown file model while adding a calmer planning loop around it.

opportunities.title: Philo vs static markdown daily notes

bestFor: People who already like markdown but want a stronger execution loop
Users who are tired of manually rebuilding context every morning
notFor: People who only need text capture and nothing else
Workflows where manual carry-forward is not a problem

faqs.question: Why not stay with plain markdown files only?
faqs.answer: You can, but plain files typically push rollover, recurrence, and tool building back onto the user every day.

faqs.question: Does Philo stop being markdown-native?
faqs.answer: No. The file on disk remains markdown; Philo changes the planning behavior around it.

Workflows that never use daily notes as the planning surface

faqs.question: Does Philo replace the daily note file model?
faqs.answer: No. Philo keeps daily notes as markdown files on disk while changing how they are surfaced in the app.

faqs.question: Why does a timeline matter?
faqs.answer: It reduces the morning reconstruction work of finding what was already in flight before you start planning.

notFor: People who prefer a strict ticket-first workflow for everything

faqs.question: What is Philo optimizing for?
faqs.answer: Philo is optimizing for a shorter loop: write, decide, and run without rebuilding context somewhere else.

faqs.question: Why does this matter?
faqs.answer: Because momentum often dies at the handoff from raw note to another separate execution tool.

faqs.question: What is the source of truth for notes in Philo?
faqs.answer: The markdown file on disk remains the source of truth for note bodies; Philo edits TipTap JSON in memory and saves back to markdown.

faqs.question: Does Philo lock you into a proprietary format?
faqs.answer: No. Philo is designed to sit beside your vault and keep notes as markdown files you control.

faqs.question: What does note chat do in Philo?
faqs.answer: Philo includes optional AI features for note chat, search, and dry-run edits inside the app.

faqs.question: Why keep chat inside the note?
faqs.answer: It keeps context attached to the planning surface instead of forcing another handoff to a separate tool.

opportunities.title: Task rollover in daily notes
opportunities.summary: What task rollover means in Philo and why it keeps daily planning lighter.
opportunities.audience: people who lose unfinished work between days
bestFor: Anyone who wants unfinished tasks to reappear automatically
Daily note users who want less manual carry-forward work
notFor: People who intentionally rebuild their task list from scratch every day
Workflows that already enforce carry-forward elsewhere

faqs.question: Is rollover the same as recurrence?
faqs.answer: No. Rollover keeps unfinished work alive, while recurrence recreates scheduled work on a cadence.

faqs.question: Why is rollover useful in markdown notes?
faqs.answer: It removes the manual copy-paste step that usually breaks momentum in plain daily note systems.

opportunities.title: Running recurring systems from daily notes
opportunities.summary: Why Philo fits people whose routines should rebuild themselves automatically.
opportunities.audience: people with recurring obligations
bestFor: People who run daily, weekly, or every-few-days routines from notes
Users who want recurrence logic to stay visible in plain text
notFor: People who only track one-off tasks
Calendar-heavy workflows that need complex scheduling rules

faqs.question: What makes recurring work feel lighter in Philo?
faqs.answer: Philo brings recurring tasks back on schedule so the planning surface rebuilds itself without manual setup every morning.

faqs.question: Why call this a system instead of a reminder?
faqs.answer: Because the value is in reducing repeat decisions, not just pinging you at a certain time.

relatedLinks.label: From recurring tasks to recurring systems

opportunities.title: Philo vs manual recurring checklists
opportunities.summary: Why automatic recurrence changes the planning burden in a markdown workflow.
opportunities.audience: people manually rewriting the same routine
bestFor: People whose checklists should rebuild themselves
Users who want recurrence visible in plain text
notFor: People who deliberately rewrite routines from scratch
Workflows that do not repeat often enough to justify automation

faqs.question: What is the real benefit of recurrence?
faqs.answer: It reduces decisions you never needed to make again, which makes the planning surface calmer.

faqs.question: Why keep recurrence inline?
faqs.answer: Because the rule stays editable where the task already lives instead of hiding in a separate settings surface.

relatedLinks.label: From recurring tasks to recurring systems

comparisonRows.question: You are rewriting the same routine every day
comparisonRows.philo: Removes that step by bringing recurring tasks back on schedule.
comparisonRows.alternative: Keeps the burden on you every time.

comparisonRows.question: You want recurrence logic visible in the task text
comparisonRows.philo: Uses inline tags inside the markdown.
comparisonRows.alternative: Usually relies on memory or copy-forward rituals.

comparisonRows.question: You prefer explicit manual control over every recurrence step
comparisonRows.philo: Less ideal if you do not want automation.

opportunities.title: Recurring task tags in Philo
opportunities.summary: How inline recurrence tags make recurring work obvious and editable in plain text.
opportunities.audience: people who run routines from daily notes
bestFor: People who want daily or weekly routines to manage themselves
Markdown users who want recurrence logic next to the task text
notFor: People who need a complex calendar-first recurrence system
Teams trying to model company-wide scheduling rules

faqs.question: What tags does Philo use?
faqs.answer: Philo uses inline recurrence tags such as #daily, #weekly, and #3days so the task carries its own recurrence rules.

faqs.question: Why use tags instead of a separate form?
faqs.answer: Tags keep the rule visible in the markdown itself, which makes the behavior easier to trust and edit.

relatedLinks.label: From recurring tasks to recurring systems

faqs.answer: Yes. Search is one of the optional AI-adjacent features available inside the app.

faqs.question: Why pair search with daily notes?
faqs.answer: It shortens the loop between finding context and deciding what to do next.
